coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots decorated cadmium sulfide nanomaterials for boosting photocatalytic activity for ciprofloxacin degradation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Choudhary, Meena
Saini, Pooja
Chakinala, Nandana
Surolia, Praveen K.
Gupta Chakinala, Anand
coredata.dc:description: This study investigates the utilization of carbon dots (CDs) from neem leaves (Azadirachta indica) decorated onto cadmium sulfide (CdS) for the photocatalytic degradation of ciprofloxacin. A comparative study of ciprofloxacin degradation with pristine CdS and CD decorated CdS demonstrated high degradation of ∼ 75 % with CD/CdS when compared to bare CdS (∼68 %). Process optimization studies were further carried out with CD/CdS catalysts at different solution pH (4–10), feed concentrations (10–50 mg/L), catalyst loadings (25–125 mg/L), temperatures (10 – 30 °C), and lamp power (25, 50, 250 W and sunlight). Higher temperatures, combined with a solution pH of 7 and catalyst loading of 100 mg/L favored the enhanced degradation of 20 mg/L of ciprofloxacin. The ciprofloxacin degradation rate increased linearly with temperature with an apparent activation energy of 27 kJ mol−1. The CD/CdS photocatalyst demonstrated maximum degradation rates with higher lamp powers while it also showed remarkable performance under natural sunlight achieving the same degradation within 3 h.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Degradation
Ciprofloxacin
Photocatalysis
Carbon dots
Cadmium Sulphide

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of different photocatalysts for the degradation of ciprofloxacin
Possible mechanistic approaches and scavenging effect of photocatalytic degradation of ciprofloxacin

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Visible absorption spectrum of (a) CD (b) CdS and CD/CdS composite catalysts.
FTIR spectra of (a) CD (b) CdS and CD/CdS composite catalysts.
PL spectra of (a) CD at different wavelengths (b) CdS and CD/CdS composite catalysts.
XRD analysis of CdS and CD/CdS composite catalysts.

Effect of different catalysts on (a) degradation profile of ciprofloxacin (b) Rate constant. [Experimental conditions: pH = 7, feed concentration = 20 mg/L, feed volume = 500 ml, catalyst loading = 100 mg/L, reaction time = 1 h, lamp power = 250 W].

Effect of feed concentration on (a) degradation profile of ciprofloxacin (b) rate constant and percentage degradation [Experimental conditions: pH = 7, feed volume = 500 ml, catalyst loading = 100 mg/L, catalyst = 1CD/CdS, reaction time = 1 h, lamp power = 250 W].

Effect of catalyst loading on (a) degradation profile of ciprofloxacin (b) rate constant and degradation [Experimental conditions: pH = 7, feed concentration = 20 mg/L, feed volume = 500 ml, lamp power = 250 W, catalyst = 1CD/CdS, reaction time = 1 h].

Effect of different pH on (a) degradation profile of ciprofloxacin (b) Zeta potential with varying pH [Experimental conditions: feed concentration = 20 mg/L, feed volume = 500 ml, catalyst loading = 100 mg/L, lamp power = 250 W, catalyst = 1CD/CdS, reaction time = 1 h].

Effect of different temperature on (a) degradation profile of ciprofloxacin (b) Arrhenius plot [Experimental conditions: pH = 7, feed concentration = 20 mg/L, feed volume = 500 ml, catalyst loading = 100 mg/L, lamp power = 250 W, catalyst = 1CD/CdS, reaction time = 1 h].

Effect of different pollutants on (a) degradation profile of organics [Experimental conditions: pH = 7, feed concentration = 20 mg/L, feed volume = 500 ml, catalyst loading = 100 mg/L, lamp power = 250 W, catalyst = 1CD/CdS, reaction time = 1 h].

Effect of different lamp powers and light sources on: (a) degradation profile of ciprofloxacin (b) obtained rate constants [Experimental conditions: pH = 7, feed concentration = 20 mg/L, feed volume = 500 ml, catalyst loading = 100 mg/L, catalyst = 1CD/CdS].

head.ce:title: Carbon dots decorated cadmium sulfide nanomaterials for boosting photocatalytic activity for ciprofloxacin degradation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study investigates the utilization of carbon dots (CDs) from neem leaves (Azadirachta indica) decorated onto cadmium sulfide (CdS) for the photocatalytic degradation of ciprofloxacin. A comparative study of ciprofloxacin degradation with pristine CdS and CD decorated CdS demonstrated high degradation of ∼ 75 % with CD/CdS when compared to bare CdS (∼68 %). Process optimization studies were further carried out with CD/CdS catalysts at different solution pH (4–10), feed concentrations (10–50 mg/L), catalyst loadings (25–125 mg/L), temperatures (10 – 30 °C), and lamp power (25, 50, 250 W and sunlight). Higher temperatures, combined with a solution pH of 7 and catalyst loading of 100 mg/L favored the enhanced degradation of 20 mg/L of ciprofloxacin. The ciprofloxacin degradation rate increased linearly with temperature with an apparent activation energy of 27 kJ mol−1. The CD/CdS photocatalyst demonstrated maximum degradation rates with higher lamp powers while it also showed remarkable performance under natural sunlight achieving the same degradation within 3 h.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Water, crucial for life, is facing quality challenges due to rising population, rapid industrialization, and increased demand in various sectors. Pharmaceuticals are the emerging contaminants often found in water bodies, which if not treated efficiently can pose significant risks to human health and aquatic systems. Current water treatment methods struggle to remove antibiotics and require the need for the development of cost effective and efficient processes. To address this, “heterogeneous photocatalysis” emerges as a promising, cost effective and efficient treatment solution which utilizes low cost semiconductor-type photocatalysts to degrade pollutants under visible light conditions. This process harnesses light energy to generate reactive oxygen species, combating water contamination effectively [1–4].

Cadmium Sulfide (CdS) semiconductor nanoparticles have gained significant attention due to their narrow band gap (2.42 eV), remarkable light harvesting capabilities, and appropriate band potential. These properties make CdS an efficient photocatalyst for applications such as hydrogen evolution and the degradation of organic dyes [5]. As a member of the II-VI group semiconductors, CdS exhibits outstanding photoelectrochemical performance and possesses a unique band structure. In this structure, 3p orbitals contribute to the top of the valence band (VB), while cadmium 5 s and 5p orbitals contribute to the bottom of the conduction band (CB). The cadmium 3d orbitals dominate the bottommost position of the CB [5]. However, the efficiency of CdS as a photocatalyst is hindered by challenges such as photogenic charge-hole recombination, photo corrosion, and particle agglomeration[6]. To address these issues, various strategies have been explored, including structural and morphological modifications, alterations in the band gap, incorporation of co-catalysts, surface sensitization, and the addition of solid support to the light harvester[7].

Among the several strategies aiming to enhance the photocatalytic performance, sensitization of quantum dots with photocatalysts was reported to be promising [11]. Incorporation of quantum dots composites have several benefits in terms of absorbing visible light, enhancing the generation of e−/h+ pairs, with ability to store photogenerated e− thereby reducing the e−/h+ recombination. Carbon dots (CD-) have emerged as a novel class of nanocarbon materials with exceptional photophysical and chemical properties. The bio-inspired synthesis methods of CD- make them a material of interest with remarkable applications in sensor fabrication, biomedical applications, and photocatalysis. The unique attributes of CD-, including their appropriate size range (<10 nm), excitation-dependent photoluminescence, photoinduced electron transfer properties, and their ability to act as an electron reservoir, contribute to their exceptional performance in photocatalytic systems [8–10]. Previous studies have highlighted the benefits of constructing heterojunctions based on CD-, showcasing the ability to direct the flow of photo-generated charge carriers. This is achieved through the electron-accepting and transport properties of CDs, leading to enhanced photocatalytic performance. Therefore, the strategic design of a novel photocatalyst becomes crucial, capitalizing on the combined advantages of CD- and CdS to achieve superior photocatalytic efficiency. For example, Xu et al. reported the combination of CdS nanoparticles with MoS2, resulting in enhanced absorption of visible light and effective separation of charge carriers (e−/h+ pairs), thereby improving photocatalytic efficiency [11]. Another study by Wang et al. probes into a CdS heterojunction based on graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), where the crystallinity of g-C3N4 is controlled to modify its photocatalytic abilities [12].

The goal of this work is to create stable CD decorated CdS nanocomposites to enhance the photocatalytic degradation of model pharmaceutical compounds (here: Ciprofloxacin). The objectives are three folds, where the first objective is to synthesize CD from waste biomass (here: neem leaves) and its further deposition on the CdS. The second objective is to evaluate the photocatalytic performance of these nanocomposites for the degradation of ciprofloxacin under various operating conditions. Last objective is to evaluate its performance under different lamp sources and compare it with different effluents. These studies have shown to be promising with CD/CdS composites for the enhanced degradation of organics.

Anhydrous cadmium chloride (99.0 %) was procured from Merck Life Science Private Limited, Sodium Sulfide (98.0 %) and ethanol (99.9 %) was purchased from Fischer Scientific India Private Ltd. Ciprofloxacin was procured from Cipla Ltd. Distilled water was used for the synthesis of all catalysts, while reverse osmosis (RO) water was used for the preparation of feed solution.

Neem leaves collected from the university premises were cleaned thoroughly using tap water and subsequently washed with distilled water to remove any particulates. These leaves were sun dried and then pulverized into a fine powder which served as a precursor for carbon dots (CD-) synthesis. Specifically, 5 g of powdered leaves was mixed with 50 ml distilled water and stirred for 1 h for proper suspension. The mixture was then transferred to a Teflon autoclave and heated at 150 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the sample was centrifuged and filtered, resulting in a brownish-colored solution indicative of the CD- which is then stored at 4 °C for their use in experiments [13].

The synthesis of CdS procedure was adapted as reported in the literature [28] wherein, 1 M solutions of CdCl2 and Na2S, each dissolved in 50 ml of distilled water and stirred for 5 min separately. Subsequently, the Na2S solution was gradually added to the CdCl2 solution, followed by continuous stirring for an additional 5 h. This process resulted in a dark yellowish solution, indicating the formation of CdS. The final product was then filtered and dried in an oven at 50 °C overnight.

The CD decorated CdS composite was prepared using the hydrothermal method, as detailed elsewhere [29]. Initially, 1 g of CdS was dispersed into 40 ml distilled water and varying amounts of carbon dots that is (1 or 2 ml of CD solution) were diluted in 20 ml of ethanol that are labeled as 1CD and 2CD. The solution mixture was sonicated using a bath sonicator (model: LMUC-12, power, 300 W) for 30 min followed by continuous stirring for 4 h. Thereafter, the solution was transferred into Teflon autoclave and heated at 150 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the sample was filtered and dried in an oven at 60 °C overnight. The final product was a dark yellow powder different from CdS and is identified as the CD/CdS composite. The composite photocatalyst synthesized using 1 ml of CD solution is referred as 1CD/CdS, while the one synthesized with 2 ml of CD solution is referred as 2CD/CdS.

The performance of the synthesized CD/CdS composite catalysts was determined for the photocatalytic degradation of ciprofloxacin under UV light. All experiments were conducted in a batch photochemical reactor that is equipped with a 500 mL reaction vessel and a 250 W mercury vapor lamp. A measured quantity of the catalyst, depending on the test was added to ciprofloxacin solution. The mixture was stirred in the dark for 30 min to reach adsorption equilibrium. Following this, the mixture was exposed to UV light while maintaining the same stirring speed. The reaction temperature was maintained at 30 °C (unless otherwise mentioned) by the forced circulation of chilled water through double jacket arrangement. Samples were periodically collected from the reactor and filtered through 0.2-μm filter that is centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant liquid was then analyzed using UV–visible spectrophotometer (HACH-DR6000) at maximum wavelength of 271 nm for ciprofloxacin.

The concentration of other pollutants namely nitrobenzene, metrogyl, malachite green dye was determined by measuring at their maximum absorbance wavelengths of 252 nm, 317 nm and 617 nm respectively. The percentage of degradation and the apparent rate constant was calculated using eq. (1) and (2) respectively.
(1)
Degradation
( % )
=
1 - C /
C t
× 100
(2)
- ln
C
C t
=
K
app
t
where C and Ct are the initial concentration and the concentration at a specific time t, respectively and Kapp is the apparent reaction rate constant calculated from the slope of ln(C/Ct) vs t.

All the catalysts synthesized in this study were characterized using various techniques to determine their functional groups, crystallinity, and band gap analysis. The structural and chemical state analysis of the catalysts was performed using X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, Make: Rigaku, Model Smart lab) in the 2θ range of 10-80° at a scan speed of 0.1 s−1. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) analysis was conducted using a Bruker ALPHA instrument and spectra was recorded from 500 to 4000 cm−1. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra was recorded using Horiba spectrometer with an integration time of 5 s to study the recombination of electron-hole in the sample. The diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) of the samples were determined using a double beam Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometer.
The synthesized CD- and CD/CdS composites were analyzed to determine their band gap, PL intensity and crystallinity as discussed in detail below.

The absorption spectra and the band gap analysis of the composites are shown in Fig. 1 a and the Fig. 1b. The absorption spectra of CD comprised of two peaks at 270 nm and 320 nm that are mainly attributed to π-π* transition of C = C bonds and n-π* transition of C = O bonds in the CD respectively[14,15].

The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the CdS and varying CD decorated CdS are shown in Fig. 1b. The Kubelka-Munk equation was employed to analyze the diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) data, following methods were reported elsewhere[16]. CD decorated on to CdS resulted in significant changes in peak shifts, leading to a slight increase in the band gap energy as compared to pristine CdS. The band gap energies of CdS and CD/CdS were found to be 2.06 and 2.24 eV respectively. No alteration in bandgap energy was noted with either 1CD/CdS or 2CD/CdS composites.

The FTIR spectra of carbon dots is displayed in Fig. 2 a, where the peaks observed in the fingerprint region of 3741–3837 cm−1 are indicative of the stretching vibrations of adsorbed hydroxyl groups, while C–H groups of alkanes is responsible for peak at 3068 and 2938 cm−1. The absorption maxima at 1573 and 1402 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibrations of C = C and O–H, respectively. Further peaks at 1075 cm−1 is indicative of stretching vibrations of the C–N groups [17].

Fig. 2b illustrates the FTIR spectra of the CD/CdS composites, where the stretching mode of Cd–S is represented by the band located at 612 and 542 cm−1. Vibrational bands at 3740 and 1696 cm−1 were observed due to the adsorbed water molecules on the CdS nanoparticles[18]. Additionally, certain bands that correspond to the functional moieties of CD, such as –OH, –NH2, C = O, C = C, along with the characteristic Cd-S stretching bands are visible in the vibrational spectra of 1CD/CdS and 2CD/CdS heterostructures confirms the presence of CD on to CdS.

The fluorescence emission spectra were shown at various excitation wavelengths between 290 and 350 nm to verify the CD exhibit excitation dependent fluorescence emission (Fig. 3 a). Initially, the emission intensity increased gradually with a red shift and the maximum excitation increased up to 320 nm. The maximum fluorescence emission intensity was reached at 320 nm of excitation wavelength along with corresponding emission at 385 nm. However, the emission intensity gradually dropped when the excitation wavelength was changed further, from 330 to 350 nm and these observations are identical to those described in the literature [13–15]. The PL spectra of CdS and 1CD/CdS catalyst are determined at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm that is shown in Fig. 3b. The broad spectrum of CdS nanoparticles lies between 400–650 nm and the spectra of CdS have one stronger peak and the spectra of CD/CdS have two absorption peaks suggesting the CD decorated on CdS.

The structural identification and phase conformation analysis of the synthesized 1CD/CdS, 2CD/CdS and pure CdS samples were confirmed using XRD diffraction patterns, as shown in Fig. 4 . The XRD peaks at 2θ values of 25, 26.5, 28.2, 43.7, 47.7, and 51.8° are indexed to the 100, 002, 101, 110, 103, and 112 diffraction planes. These findings are in good agreement with the hexagonal phase which is indexed for CdS hexagonal wurtzite phase reported in existing literature [4,19]. The CdS and CD/CdS diffraction patterns also show comparable peak positions without any shifting, indicating that the CdS crystallinity is not affected by the incorporation of CD [20].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different photocatalysts for the degradation of ciprofloxacin
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of various photocatalysts (CD, CdS, and CD/CdS composites) in degrading ciprofloxacin is illustrated in Fig. 5 . Degradation profiles are presented in Fig. 5a, and their corresponding rate constants are tabulated in Fig. 5b. The initial photolysis test, conducted without any catalyst, showed the least degradation. However, with the addition of photocatalysts it significantly enhanced the degradation. The degradation efficiency of CD and CdS was observed to be 51 % and 68 %, respectively, with rate constants of 0.0154 min−1 and 0.0223 min−1. Notably, the sensitization of CD with CdS resulted in increased degradation efficiency, reaching approximately 73% and 74 % with 1CD/CdS and 2CD/CdS, accompanied by rate constants of 0.0298 min−1 and 0.0281 min−1, respectively. Consequently, further optimization studies were conducted using the 1CD/CdS catalyst.

Recent research has highlighted the improved photocatalytic performance of CD/CdS nanowires, particularly in the degradation of different dyes [4,20,21]. This enhancement is attributed to the increased carrier density within the heterojunction and the enhanced electronic transmission capacity [20]. Moreover, the CD@CdS catalytic system has shown enhanced rates of hydrogen evolution during photocatalytic water splitting, alongside enhanced degradation of crystal violet dye. These improvements are credited to the oxygen-rich hydrophilic surface properties of the CD and its favorable band structure. The collaborative action of CdS as a light harvester and CD in reducing the recombination has significantly boosted the photocatalytic effectiveness of the heterojunction[22]. Earlier studies on CD-doped CdS microspheres have also reported enhanced removal of Rh-B dye due to heightened carrier density and diminished fluorescence after incorporation of CD in to CdS [23]. These findings demonstrate the effectiveness of CD/CdS composites for photocatalytic degradation of wastewater pollutants.

The impact of feed concentration on ciprofloxacin degradation was studied within the range of 10–50 mg/L, with a catalyst loading of 100 mg/L as illustrated in Fig. 6 . It is apparent that as the feed concentration increases, there was a noteworthy decrease in degradation efficiency. Optimal degradation of 88 % was attained at a concentration of 10 mg/L, accompanied by a corresponding rate constant of 0.0527 min−1. As the feed concentration exceeded 10 mg/L, degradation percentages of 75, 46, and 36 % were observed for 20, 40, and 50 mg/L ciprofloxacin concentration, respectively, with corresponding rate constants of 0.02984, 0.0138, and 0.0107 min−1. This decline in degradation rate at higher feed concentrations can be attributed to the reduced availability of catalytically active sites in relation to the feed concentration[4].

The impact of varying catalyst loadings (25 to 125 mg/L) on the photocatalytic degradation of ciprofloxacin (20 mg/L) is illustrated in Fig. 7 . From Fig. 7a and 7b, it was observed that with increase in catalyst loading from 25 to 125 mg/L, the degradation rate increased from 63 % to 75 % with a corresponding increase in rate from 0.02207 min−1 to 0.0316 min−1. The increased degradation with high catalyst loadings may be attributed to the generation of more oxidizing radicals, facilitated by an increased availability of active sites. These reactive species are more prevalent at lower concentrations of ciprofloxacin due to the competition between the catalyst's active sites and ciprofloxacin particles. This insight suggests a potential optimization strategy for achieving enhanced photocatalytic performance in the degradation of ciprofloxacin [39]. Comparable outcomes demonstrating improved degradation with higher catalyst loadings were reported in the photocatalytic degradation of ciprofloxacin, facilitated by silver nanoparticles supported on amorphous silica, achieving optimal effectiveness at a catalyst loading of 0.12 g/L [1]. Similar observations of enhanced photocatalytic degradation of methyl paraben was reported using a graphene oxide/cadmium sulfide composite, with an identified optimal concentration of 0.75 g/L [3].

The solution pH plays a vital role in determining the efficiency and effectiveness of the wastewater treatment processes as it controls the interaction between pollutants and the photocatalyst. The effect of solution pH on the degradation of ciprofloxacin was studied by varying pH from 4 to 10 as presented in Fig. 8 . The degradation rate of ciprofloxacin is lower at acidic milieu (pH: 4.0 – 6.0), maximizing at neutral and alkaline conditions (pH: 7 and 10).

Ciprofloxacin exhibits distinct behavior at different pH levels, understanding its charge state and interactions with photocatalysts is crucial for efficient degradation. With pKa values of approximately 5.9 or 6.1 and 8.89, ciprofloxacin exhibits both acidic and basic properties. It can exist as a cation, zwitterion, or anion depending on the pH of the environment.

The PZC (Point of Zero Charge) of the CD/CdS nanocomposite, as shown in Fig. 8b, is measured at 4. Below pH 4, the nanocomposite surface carries a positive charge, while above pH 4, it becomes negatively charged due to an excess of OH– ions in the solution. Ciprofloxacin is a zwitterionic molecule containing both positively and negatively charged functional groups within the same molecule. At pH below 6.1, the carboxylic acid group tends to be protonated (–COOH), leading to a slightly positive charge on the molecule under acidic conditions, while the –NH- group remains unprotonated. Since the nanocomposite also carries a positive charge under these conditions, repulsive interactions between the positively charged catalyst surfaces and ciprofloxacin reduce degradation efficiency at low pH. Conversely, at pH above 8.9, the –COOH group is unprotonated, while the –NH- group remains unprotonated or partially protonated, contributing to an overall negatively charged form of the ciprofloxacin molecule. Consequently, lower degradation efficiency is observed under basic conditions due to repulsive forces between like charges of the surface and the ciprofloxacin molecule. However, at pH levels between 6 and 8.9, the ciprofloxacin molecule exists in its zwitterionic form, while the surface of the nanocomposite is negatively charged. This allows for electrostatic interactions between the negatively charged surface and the partially charged ciprofloxacin molecules, facilitating the adsorption of ciprofloxacin onto the catalyst surface. As a result, photocatalytic degradation of ciprofloxacin is maximized at pH 7.[24–26].

The effect of different temperatures on the degradation profile of ciprofloxacin was studied by varying from 10 – 30 °C and the kinetics is shown in Fig. 9 . As seen from Fig. 9a, the degradation rate increased from 0.013 to 0.030 min−1 with increase in temperature. Generally, in heterogeneous photocatalysis there is an inherent relation between both thermodynamic and kinetic aspects. Increasing temperature promotes a rapid and more efficient interfacial transfer and separation rate of photogenerated carriers, resulting in a reduced recombination rate of electron/hole pairs. Concurrently, there is an enhanced generation rate of free radicals (both •O2 – and •OH), leading to a significant elevation in the overall photocatalytic activity of CD/CdS[27].

Chemical reactions are generally adhered to the Arrhenius law (3), in which Ea is apparent activation energy (kJ/mol), R is the gas constant, A is a pre-exponential factor (min−1).
(3)
lnk =
E a
/ R T + l n A
The apparent activation energy of ciprofloxacin was determined to be 27 kJ mol−1 suggesting the degradation behavior to controlled by diffusion due to the dominance of free radical reactions. These findings align closely with previous observations where activation energy of 17.5 kJ mol−1 was reported with the sonolysis of ciprofloxacin[28]. Whereas, the ciprofloxacin degradation was found to have an activation energy of 69.7 kJ mol−1 with persulfate activated carbon and cobalt dual catalyst[29]. Photocatalytic degradation of amoxicillin trihydrate with Sn/Zn/TiO2 nanoparticles had an activation energy of 18.0 kJ mol−1 [30]. Catalytic wet air oxidation of ciprofloxacin was reported to have an activation energy of 53.8 kJ mol−1 [31]. An activation energy of 10.8 kJ mol−1 was reported for the sono-catalytic degradation of tylosin using WS2/CeO2 [32]. Photocatalytic degradation of enrofloxacin with bimetallic CdS/CuAg photocatalyst under visible light was found to have a very low activation energy of 0.183 kJ mol−1 [33]. Bismuth Ferrite catalyzed photo-degradation of Rhodamine-B was reported to have an activation energy of 25.8 kJ mol−1 [34]. Overall, it can be inferred that temperature has a noteworthy impact on both the kinetic and thermodynamic aspects. Where, elevated temperatures enhance the interactions between free radicals and pollutant molecules, thereby accelerating the production of free radicals.

Understanding the catalyst performance with various pollutants provides several insights for potential real-world application in wastewater treatment processes. To investigate the effectiveness of CD/CdS, studies were carried out under identical conditions with various other pollutants such as nitrobenzene, metrogyl, malachite green, real textile effluent shown in Fig. 10 . It was seen that the maximum degradation rate (0.0298 min−1) was achieved with ciprofloxacin and the lowest rate was obtained with both real textile effluent and nitrobenzene (0.0021 & 0.0070 min−1 respectively). The catalyst performance was moderate enough in terms of metrogyl and malachite green degradation where the rate was found to be 0.0126 and 0.0233 min−1. Similar observations of different photocatalytic degradation behavior of organic pollutants having various functional groups was reported[35,36]. It is crucial to note that the photocatalytic degradation rate of a specific pollutant is intricately influenced by its inherent characteristics, concentration levels, and the coexistence of other compounds within the water matrix[35,37]. Thus, it is essential to tailor the application of the catalyst for real world applications.

The degradation efficiency in photocatalysis is intricately associated with the light power source. The majority of photocatalytic studies were reported utilizing high-power light sources to enhance photocatalytic degradation performance. Nevertheless, these high lamp power sources necessitate considerable energy consumption and produce substantial heat, requiring continuous heat dissipation from the system, consequently increasing the treatment cost. Catalysts exhibiting high activity at low lamp power sources are competitive from a practical application both in terms of energy conservation and environmental sustainability. Therefore, the impact of varying lamp power conditions (25 to 250 W) on the photocatalytic degradation of ciprofloxacin at a catalyst loading of 100 mg/L is studied as illustrated in Fig. 11 . It was found that lower degradation of about 30–40 % was achieved using lower lamp powers of 25–50 W. Maximum degradation of ∼ 73 % was achieved with 250 W lamp power. The enhanced degradation at high lamp powers is due to the enhanced photon absorption and generation of higher quantum of hydroxyl radicals affecting the degradation process[16]. Although, the degradation rate is relatively lower under natural sunlight when compared to UV lamp powers, maximum degradation of 73 % was achieved after 3 h treatment time. Overall, the catalyst exhibited commendable photocatalytic activity in the presence of visible light conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanistic approaches and scavenging effect of photocatalytic degradation of ciprofloxacin
ce:section.ce:para:
The general mechanistic approach for the degradation of ciprofloxacin with a photocatalyst in the presence of UV/visible light spectrum is explained in the reactions (4) – (11) given below.
(4) CD/CdS + hυ → CD/CdS (e−, h+)
(5) h+ + Ciprofloxacin → Degradation products (Major reaction)
(6) e−+ O2 → O−
2
(7) O−
2+ Ciprofloxacin → Degradation products (Moderate reaction)
(8) O−
2 + H+ aq → HO
2
(9)
2 H O
2
∙
→
H 2
O
2
+
O 2
(10)
H 2
O 2
+
e
cb
-
→
OH
∙
+
OH
-
(11)
OH
∙
/
h
VB
-
+ C i p r o f l o x a c i n → D e g r a d a t i o n
p r o d u c t s
Firstly, the pollutant adsorbs on the surface of the photocatalyst after being activated by an appropriate photon, producing charge carriers electrons (e−) and holes (h+). As a result, ciprofloxacin is oxidized by the h+ by direct interaction (Eq. (5). In contrast, oxygen may absorb photogenerated (e−) and convert it into a superoxide radical anion (Eq. (6). Additionally, the superoxide radical may convert to generate hydroperoxyl radicals, hydrogen peroxide, and hydroxyl radicals (equations (8)–(10). Overall, the degradation of ciprofloxacin to final breakdown products with the reactive species in aqueous media is described in Eq. (11).

To determine the role of radical species that dominate the photocatalytic degradation of ciprofloxacin, studies were carried out with different radical trapping agents such as isopropanol (IPA), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) and sodium oxalate. IPA acts as an effective scavenger for hydroxyl radicals, while EDTA and sodium oxalate acts as a hole scavenger. Results show that in the absence of any scavenger, ciprofloxacin degradation reached 78 %. However, upon the addition of radical trapping substances such as EDTA (40 mmol/L), IPA (40 mmol/L), and sodium oxalate (40 mmol/L), the percentage of ciprofloxacin degradation decreased to 18.9 %, 65.7 %, and 39.1 %, respectively. Overall, this suggests that holes are the most active species in the photocatalytic degradation of ciprofloxacin.

This study deals with the photocatalytic degradation of ciprofloxacin as a model pollutant in the presence of carbon dot decorated CdS. The incorporation of CD in the CdS led to structural defects, which played a key role in achieving enhanced photocatalytic degradation of ciprofloxacin under UV light compared to that of pristine CdS. The mechanism involved in the photocatalytic degradation of ciprofloxacin has been elucidated involving effect of different catalysts, pH adjustments, feed concentration and catalyst loading. Maximum degradation was achieved at low feed concentrations, neutral solution pH, and high catalyst loadings. The catalyst was found to be highly active at high UV lamp powers and is shown to be promising even under visible light conditions. Additionally, the catalyst demonstrated different degradation behavior with various other organic pollutants depending on the type and functionalities. Overall, CD decorated CdS is shown to be a promising photocatalyst for the degradation of pharmaceutical effluents for sustainable water treatment.

Meena Choudhary: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Pooja Saini: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Formal analysis, Data curation. Nandana Chakinala: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision. Praveen K. Surolia: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources. Anand Gupta Chakinala: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Manipal University Jaipur (MUJ) for providing access to the Central Analytical Facilities (CAF) and Sophisticated Analytical Instrumentation Facility (SAIF). PKS would like to acknowledge the funding support from Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB), India under grant No. [CRG/2021/002477-G]. AGC and PKS would also like to thank the Manipal Research Board (MRB) seed grant.
